1: This is the third time (tritēn tautēn) I am coming to you — every charge must be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses (Deuteronomy 19:15) — Paul applies the Mosaic legal standard (stoma dyoin martyrōn kai triōn stathēsetai pan rhēma) to his pattern of visits: the three visits function as the three-witness testimony.
2: I warned those who sinned before and all the others, and I warn them now while absent — the double warning (past/present) mirrors the legal requirement for formal warning before executing judgment. If I come again I will not spare them (ou pheisomai) — the language of judicial severity.
3: Since you seek proof (dokimēn, a test, proof of genuineness) that Christ is speaking in me — dokimē (proof through testing) is Paul's word for authenticated character (2 Corinthians 2:9; 2 Corinthians 8:2; 2 Corinthians 9:13). He who is not weak toward you (eis hymas) but is powerful (dynatos) in you.
4: For he was crucified in weakness (ex astheneias), but lives by the power of God (ek dynameōs theou) — the cross/resurrection antithesis that runs through 2 Corinthians: weakness is the mode of crucifixion; power is the mode of resurrection. For we also are weak in him, but in dealing with you we will live with him by the power of God.
5: Examine yourselves (heautous peirazete), to see whether you are in the faith — test yourselves (heautous dokimazete) — the twin imperatives use both peirazō (test, tempt) and dokimazō (assay, evaluate); the distinction is that dokimazō implies the expectation of approval. Or do you not realize this about yourselves, that Jesus Christ is in you (en hymin)? — unless indeed you fail to meet the test (adokimoi este).
6: I hope you will find out that we have not failed the test (hoti hēmeis ouk esmen adokimoi) — the word adokimos (failing the test, disqualified) appeared in 1 Corinthians 9:27 for Paul's fear of his own disqualification; here he applies it to the Corinthians' evaluation of him.
7: But we pray to God that you may not do wrong (kakōn mē poiēsai) — not that we may appear to have met the test (hina hēmeis dokimoi phanōmen), but that you may do what is right (kalon poiēte), though we may seem to have failed — the selflessness of the pastoral prayer: Paul would rather appear adokimos himself than have the Corinthians fail morally.
8: For we cannot do anything against the truth (kata tēs alētheias), but only for the truth (hyper tēs alētheias) — the epistemological constraint: Paul's apostolic authority is bounded by truth itself; he cannot act against it regardless of personal advantage.
9: For we are glad when we are weak and you are strong (dynatos ēte) — the inversion of conventional success metrics: Paul rejoices in his own weakness when it accompanies the congregation's strength. Your restoration (katartisis, mending, fitting together — the same root as katartizō in 2 Corinthians 13:11) is what we pray for.
10: For this reason I write these things while I am away from you, that when I come I may not have to be severe (apotomōs, sharply, with cutting severity) — apotomōs (sharply, from apotemnō, to cut off) was used for abrupt/harsh action. In my use of the authority (exousia) the Lord has given me for building up (oikodomēn) and not for tearing down (kathairesis) — the building/tearing-down polarity echoes Jeremiah 1:10.
11: Finally, brothers, rejoice (chairete). Aim for restoration (katartizesthe, be restored, be mended). Comfort one another (parakaleisthe). Agree with one another (to auto phroneite). Live in peace (eirēneuete) — the five closing imperatives: joy, restoration, mutual comfort, like-mindedness, peace. And the God of love and peace will be with you.
12: Greet one another with a holy kiss (philēmati hagiō) — the holy kiss appears in all four Pauline letter-closing greetings where it occurs (Romans 16:16; 1 Corinthians 16:20; 1 Thessalonians 5:26; here). All the saints greet you.
13: All the saints greet you — the expansion of the greeting to include all the saints situates the letter in the broader fellowship of the universal church; the local letter is a communication within a network of interconnected communities.
14: The grace (charis) of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love (agapē) of God and the fellowship (koinōnia) of the Holy Spirit be with you all — the most explicitly trinitarian benediction in the Pauline corpus. Three divine persons are named with three distinct attributes: Christ's grace (the gift freely given through the cross), God's love (the eternal source of the gift), and the Spirit's koinōnia (the shared participation that binds the community to God and to one another).
